item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Location: Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill, 1525 11th Ave Suite 100, Seattle, WA

Five Iron Golf is your College Basketball HQ this March! Gather your crew and book 1–2 simulator bays for up to 12 people \(6 people per bay\) for a bracket-busting watch party experience. Enjoy all-day TV coverage of college basketball alongside cutting-edge golf and multisport simulators, a locally-inspired menu, and custom beverage packages. Book your watch party for March 19 or 20 and you'll automatically be entered to win a 20-person happy hour with drinks and appetizers — a $1,000 value! Group packages can include reserved event space, team-specific décor, a dedicated staff member, full-screen and/or picture-in-picture viewing, a customizable menu, beverage packages, free club rentals, multisport simulators, and table games. Select the "Small Event" option at booking to ensure simulator bays are placed side-by-side. Availability of select features varies by location. Promotions: Happy Hour – "It's 5i o'Clock Somewhere" \(Monday–Friday, 3–6 PM\) Enjoy $5 bottomless boneless wings, $10 bottomless bone-in wings, $5 select beers/cans/cocktails, $5 dips \(guacamole, hot honey ricotta, hummus\), half-off pizzas, and $5 featured shots. Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required. 5i After Dark – "Swing In, For Less"  \(Sunday–Thursday 9 PM to close | Friday–Saturday 10 PM to close\) Features $29/hr simulator rentals plus the same great food and drink specials as Happy Hour, with wings priced per dozen rather than bottomless. Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

Please contact healthyprograms@stjohns.ca for further information.

item.title: Working Together to Protect and Transfer Assets - VIRTUAL WEBINAR
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 11 AM

Description ️ Working Together to Protect and Transfer Assets Presented by: Peggy Haslach & Patrice Kreider-Hughes Location: GSBA Office Date & Time: March 18 11am - 12:30pm Join GSBA for an informative session designed to help LGBTQ\+ individuals, couples, and families protect what matters most. Working Together to Protect and Transfer Assets brings together a financial planner and an estate planning attorney to show how collaboration ensures your wishes are honored, your assets are protected, and your loved ones are cared for. What You'll Learn How to build a trusted planning team that understands LGBTQ\+ financial and legal needs Strategies to protect assets during your lifetime with coordinated insurance, investments, and legal planning Essential documents: durable powers of attorney, healthcare directives, wills, and trusts Aligning beneficiary designations with legal structures for seamless asset transfer Best practices across diverse family structures and varying state laws Featured Speakers Peggy Haslach, CFP, CLU Financial Planner working with women professionals and small business owners Peggy built her practice serving women professionals \(including veterinarians, doctors, and attorneys\) and small business owners. After seeing first-hand how life events impact women's financial security, she pivoted careers at 53-focused on collaborative, educational planning for women and LGBTQ\+ clients. Her clients are forward-thinking women who've put the pieces together-business, life, goals, beliefs, and savings-but want a cohesive plan they fully understand. Outside of work, Peggy competes nationally in dog agility and shares that passion with her wife, Desiree, a national and world champion handler and trainer. They live in Sumner, Washington with their three Border Collies-Vista, Cru, and Peak. Patrice M. Kreider-Hughes, Esq. Founder & Principal, Kreider Hughes Law, PLLC Patrice is an estate planning and small-business attorney with 15\+ years of experience. She earned her B.A. in psychology from the University of Virginia and her J.D. from Seattle University School of Law. Before founding Kreider Hughes Law in 2017, she served as in-house counsel for Conover Insurance, handling mergers and acquisitions, contracts, and employment matters. Featured on the Seattle Connected podcast and recognized by Expertise.com in 2023 and 2024, Patrice lives in Seattle with her wife, their daughter Simone, and pets Luther and Emma. Session Outline Why Collaboration Matters Financial planners and estate attorneys working in tandem to build resilient, values-aligned plans. Building Your Planning Team Roles, responsibilities, and coordination between advisors who understand LGBTQ\+ needs. Protecting Assets During Your Lifetime Insurance \(life, disability, long-term care\), investment strategy \(diversification, ESG\), and ownership/titling alignment. Legal Documents That Safeguard Your Wishes Durable POA, healthcare POA & advance directive, wills & trusts, and syncing beneficiary designations. Coordinating Asset Transfers Real estate titling, retirement & insurance beneficiaries, business succession, and philanthropic giving. Navigating Legal Landscapes & Family Structures Marriage recognition, parental rights, cross-border/visa considerations, and state-by-state variability. Q&A \(15 min\) - Bring your questions on real estate, trusts, chosen family, and children's inheritance planning. Who Should Attend LGBTQ\+ individuals, couples, and families seeking long-term financial security Small business owners planning for succession and legacy Anyone looking to align legal and financial strategies

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

item.title: UW Psychology Distinguished Alumni Lecture, Feat. Dr. Cynthia Berg
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 3:30 PM

Location: UW Hans Rosling Center for Population Health, 3980 15th Ave NE, Seattle, WA

University of Washington Department of Psychology - Distinguished Alumni Lecture, Feat. Dr. Cynthia Berg A Self- and Social-Regulation Perspective on Type 1 Diabetes Management Across the Life Span Type 1 diabetes is a difficult illness to manage and requires daily self-regulation \(e.g., planning to meet diabetes goals, self-efficacy, and managing emotions\) as well as social regulation \(disclosing to others to obtain needed assistance\). Although aspects of parent involvement in diabetes management decline across adolescence and into young adulthood, our longitudinal studies show that having family members and romantic partners who are knowledgeable about one’s diabetes behaviors and are available to help when needed is associated with better diabetes outcomes. Social regulation can also facilitate more effective use of technologies such as continuous glucose monitors by sharing GM data with others. These studies have served as the foundation for randomized clinical trials that hold promise for optimizing self- and social-regulation on a daily basis for individuals across the life span.

Event details

item.title: Audubon Bird Walk
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: Bellevue Botanical Garden, 12001 Main St, Bellevue, WA

Learn more about the birds at the Bellevue Botanical Garden. Join Eastside Audubon for a monthly walk that explores the birds and their habitat in the Garden. This one-mile walk will be on trails with various surfaces and may include gravel, mulch, or pavement. Please dress for the weather and wear comfortable walking shoes. Parking in the lot is free. From the parking lot enter the garden through the breezeway and turn left. Meet your guide in the covered Gathering Place. This walk is for any level of birder and no experience is necessary. Bring binoculars if you have them. There will be some available to loan for the walk. Each walk is $5 per person. Registration is required.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Mindfulness Walk | Pioneer Park
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 11 AM

Mindfulness Walk Taking inspiration from the Japanese idea of shinrin-yoku \(forest bathing\), we will be hosting a series of mindfulness walks in Pioneer Park. Mindfulness walking in the forest has shown to have a myriad of benefits from improving heart health to reducing stress \(https://www.npr.org/2023/08/22/1195337204/a-guide-to-forest-bathing\). Forest Steward Marilyn Cornwell \(https://www.soulscapes.us/\) will be lea ding an hour-long guided walk through the park. Please join us if your cur ious about mindfulness, want to learn more about and experience healing powers of being in nature, and/or if you want to deepen your connection with Pioneer Park and others. When: Thursday, March 19th, 11am-12pm Where: Pioneer Park NW, Meet at the corner of Island Crest Way and SE 68th St. Follow VOLUNTEER EVENT Signs What to bring: Dress for the weather in clothes and shoes you will comfortable walking and standing in, as well as water. Registration: https://volunteersignup.org/9XTJA

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Immigration is Not Invasion: Dean's Luminaries in Law Series with Ilya Somin
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Sullivan Hall, C5, Seattle, WA

Seattle University School of Law’s Luminaries in Law Series continues with a timely and thought-provoking conversation titled Immigration Is Not Invasion, featuring renowned constitutional law scholar Ilya Somin. In this special lunchtime event, Professor Somin will be interviewed by Anthony E. Varona, Dean of Seattle University School of Law, and Andrew Siegel, Professor of Law. Together, they will explore Professor Somin’s scholarship challenging the increasingly common framing of immigration as an “invasion,” examining its constitutional, historical, and democratic implications. The discussion will engage questions at the intersection of immigration law, constitutional structure, political rhetoric, and civil liberties, offering insight into how legal doctrine and public discourse shape immigration policy and democratic values. Drawing on their respective expertise, Dean Varona and Professor Siegel will guide a wide-ranging conversation designed to illuminate one of the most pressing legal and societal debates of our time. Students, faculty, and members of the broader legal community are invited to attend this engaging installment of the Luminaries in Law series. Date: March 19th Location: Room C5, Sullivan Hall Time: 12:00 PM to 12:50 PM.

Event details

item.title: Free Virtual Sessions for Adoption
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 12 PM

Northwest Adoption Exchange \(NWAE\) is inviting all those interested in adopting from foster care to attend free, virtual information sessions to learn about the process. Offered in partnership with the Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families \(DCYF\), these sessions provide guidance and clarity for anyone beginning or continuing their adoption journey. Who: Open to anyone interested in adopting from foster care in Washington state. What: Free virtual information sessions on the adoption process. When: Monthly sessions, participants are encouraged to attend Session 1 before attending Session 2.  Upcoming dates: ·         Session 1: March 5 at 12 p.m. or 6 p.m. ·         Session 2: March 19 at 12 p.m. or 6 p.m. Where: Online, register at nwae.org Cost: Free Led by experienced child welfare professionals, NWAE’s sessions are presented virtually in two parts and offer a comprehensive introduction to adoption. Participants will gain insights into the needs of youth waiting for permanent families and learn how to navigate each stage of the adoption journey with confidence. Session 1: Introduction to adoption from foster care Explore how adoption fits into the foster care system, the types of support available, and what to consider when determining if adoption is right for your family. Session 2: The steps to adoption Walk through the practical aspects of adoption, from choosing an agency and completing required training to navigating the home study process.

Event details

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

Event details

item.description:

Interested in a graduate program at Albers? Have questions about the admissions process? Want to connect with a current student or faculty member from your program of interest? During our graduate information sessions we take a deep dive into the following programs offered by the Albers School of Business and Economics: Early Career MBAProfessional MBA \(in-person program only\)Sport and Entertainment Management MBAMaster of Professional Accounting \(STEM, for non-accounting majors\)Master of Science in Accounting & Analytics \(STEM, for accounting majors\)Master of Science in Business Analytics \(in-person program only\) \(STEM\)Master of Science in Finance \(STEM\) We provide detailed information such as program structure, prerequisites, application information, admissions processes, scholarships, and more. To participate with video, you will need access to a device with a camera \(laptop, smart phone, etc.\). A Zoom account is not required. You may also choose to participate through audio only. Albers has separate recruiting events for the following: Online: MBA, MSBA, Cybersecurity Leadership programs Leadership Executive programs including the MBA and Certificate Please email albersgradinfo@seattleu.edu for more information.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: March Madness Watch Party at Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill, 1525 11th Ave Suite 100, Seattle, WA

Five Iron Golf is your College Basketball HQ this March! Gather your crew and book 1–2 simulator bays for up to 12 people \(6 people per bay\) for a bracket-busting watch party experience. Enjoy all-day TV coverage of college basketball alongside cutting-edge golf and multisport simulators, a locally-inspired menu, and custom beverage packages. Book your watch party for March 19 or 20 and you'll automatically be entered to win a 20-person happy hour with drinks and appetizers — a $1,000 value! Group packages can include reserved event space, team-specific décor, a dedicated staff member, full-screen and/or picture-in-picture viewing, a customizable menu, beverage packages, free club rentals, multisport simulators, and table games. Select the "Small Event" option at booking to ensure simulator bays are placed side-by-side. Availability of select features varies by location. Promotions: Happy Hour – "It's 5i o'Clock Somewhere" \(Monday–Friday, 3–6 PM\) Enjoy $5 bottomless boneless wings, $10 bottomless bone-in wings, $5 select beers/cans/cocktails, $5 dips \(guacamole, hot honey ricotta, hummus\), half-off pizzas, and $5 featured shots. Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required. 5i After Dark – "Swing In, For Less"  \(Sunday–Thursday 9 PM to close | Friday–Saturday 10 PM to close\) Features $29/hr simulator rentals plus the same great food and drink specials as Happy Hour, with wings priced per dozen rather than bottomless. Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required.

item.title: Forterra Fridays: March 2026
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 1 PM

Location: Crestview Park, 16200 42nd Avenue South, Tukwila, WA

Forterra Fridays: March 2026 Friday, Mar. 20, 2026, 1:00 pm to 3:30 pm We’re excited to invite you to volunteer with us at Crestview Park, a beloved community gathering space in Tukwila featuring dog play areas, trails, and natural landscapes. Join us on March 20, from 1:00-3:30 PM, to restore essential habitat by removing ivy and Himalayan blackberries that threaten our local native trees, shrubs, and groundcover. Established native plants benefit local wildlife, improve water quality, and enhance air quality.  You will also gain valuable insights into ecological restoration and our innovative approach to land conservation. We’ll start with a walk of the site and have restoration professionals on hand to answer questions about plants, restoration, and habitat management.  What we are doing: Crestview Park provides critical habitat restoration opportunities alongside its popular amenities. By removing invasive ivy and blackberries, we ensure that native plants can thrive, supporting the park’s ecological health while maintaining the community spaces that make this park so valued by Tukwila residents.  What to bring: Please dress for the weather \(in layers to stay warm/dry\). Snacks, drinks, tools, and gloves will all be provided. Where to park: Park in the Crestview Park parking lot \(16200 42nd Avenue South\). Signs direct you from the parking lot to where we will meet. Date and Time: Friday, March 20, 2026, 1:00 –3:30 PM  Register and learn more here.

item.title: Docent-Led Museum Tours
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 1 PM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

**Dive deeper into Nordic culture, art, and history with a guided tour led by our knowledgeable docents!** Explore the culture and values of the Nordic region across 12,000 years and 1.3 million square miles in our core exhibition, *Nordic Journeys*. Along the way, you will meet ancient nomadic and contemporary semi-nomadic peoples, Vikings, explorers, emigrants, and expatriates — all with a story to tell. These monthly drop-in tours are included with Museum admission. When you arrive, please check-in with your ticket at the Admissions Desk. Tours meet outside of Osberg Great Hall, near the entrance to the Museum. item.title: The Taste of Iceland Festival March 19-21
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Taste of Iceland returns to Seattle from Thursday, March 19, through Saturday, March 21. The cultural festival celebrates Iceland with events across the city showcasing the best of Icelandic culture, including food, music, nature, art, travel, and more. Most events are free and open to the public, including two free concerts, a prix fixe Icelandic dining experience, a comedy show, and a cocktail class. Tickets are required and guests are encouraged to arrive promptly to guarantee entry. Tickets are available on the Taste of Iceland Seattle website.  Taste of Iceland events include: Thursday, March 19 - Saturday, March 21 Icelandic Menu at Palace Kitchen: Ready to taste a plate full of Iceland? Icelandic Chef Ísak Aron Jóhannsson and Palace Kitchen Chef Ron Anderson will prepare a feast inspired by Iceland’s natural beauty and local flavors, including cod from Icelandic Seafood and lamb from Icelandic Lamb. This pop-up dining experience is available each evening beginning at 5:00 PM at Palace Kitchen. Reservations for the prix fixe menu are available at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/icelandic-menu. Wheel of Prizes Presented by Icelandair: Select guests will have the opportunity to spin the Wheel of Prizes for a chance to win amazing prizes from all festival partners during multiple events, including a trip for two to Iceland! Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/wheel-of-prizes. Thursday, March 19 Reykjavík Has Its Own Soundtrack: For one night only, Icelandic multi-instrumentalist, producer, and musician DJ Hermigervill invites Seattle audiences to experience an intimate listening party at Shibuya HiFi. The evening explores the songs that capture what it means to live and grow up in Iceland’s vibrant capital. Hermigervill opens his personal archive for an exclusive journey through Reykjavík’s musical identity, sharing rare recordings and the stories behind them. Presented in the spirit of Reykjavík Music City, the event celebrates a capital where the city helps shape the sound. 7:30 - 9:00 PM at Shibuya HiFi. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/reykjavik-soundtrack. Friday, March 20 Across Iceland: A Guided Cocktail Journey: During this two-hour cocktail class, storyteller and Icelandic guide Ragnar Bragason will lead guests through some of Iceland’s most stunning regions, sharing tales from the road and the places where Iceland Hotel Collection by BERJAYA welcomes travelers. At each stop, Icelandic mixologist, Arnór Gísli, will teach guests to create a signature cocktail inspired by the region's landscape, mood, and character. Three destinations. Three drinks. One unforgettable trip. 4:00 - 6:00 PM at Life on Mars. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/cocktail-journey. Northern Lights in Seattle: Learn about Iceland’s fascinating northern lights—which are expected to shine even brighter in 2026 and 2027—with astronomy and science educator, Sævar "Astro-Sævar" Helgi Bragason, hosted by Icelandia. Sævar will explain why Iceland is the perfect place to view the August 12, 2026, solar eclipse and recommend his favorite tours in Iceland. Guests will also be invited to experience Iceland through virtual reality, with a focus on Icelandia's Northern Lights tours, Glacier Hikes, and ATV tours. 6:00 - 7:30 PM at the National Nordic Museum. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/northern-lights. Iceland Airwaves Off-Venue: Enjoy a free concert curated by Iceland Airwaves, an award-winning music festival in Reykjavík, featuring RAKEL and Spacestation, with DJ Hermigervill spinning Icelandic tunes between sets. RAKEL’s intimate, atmospheric music combines voice and emotion into something quietly powerful. Spacestation blends shimmering rock and textured synths, creating a cosmic energy that feels both nostalgic and fresh. DJ Hermigervill is a multi-instrumentalist, producer, composer, and DJ. Doors open at 7:00 PM at Conor Byrne Co-op. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/iceland-airwaves-off-venue. Saturday, March 21 Draw Iceland’s Stories! Family Workshop with Illustrator Rán Flygenring: Join award-winning Icelandic storyteller and illustrator Rán Flygenring for a playful, inspiring family art workshop where imagination meets Icelandic nature and history. In this workshop, Rán invites children and parents alike to explore storytelling through drawing, doodling, and other creative activities. Families will learn about Iceland’s powerful landscapes, trailblazing leaders, and creative spirit. All ages are welcome, and no prior art experience is required—just curiosity, imagination, and a sense of fun! 11:00 AM - 2:00 PM at the National Nordic Museum. Sign up at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/draw-icelands-stories for a chance to win a signed edition of one of Rán's illustrated children’s books. Iceland Unfiltered: Get ready for an evening of laughter hosted by Icelandair! At this one-night-only comedy event, two Icelanders are flying to Seattle to reveal the quirky truths, cultural oddities, and straight-up strange realities of Iceland. Comedians and storytellers Ragnar Bragason and Bergur Ebbi will share hilarious personal stories, offer wildly unqualified advice, and roast what tourists get wrong…every single time. Each guest will receive a complimentary cocktail infused with local flavors and Blue Lagoon Iceland will provide every guest with a skincare gift. Expect laughter and at least five conversation-starters for your next dinner party. Spoiler: You've definitely been pronouncing Reykjavík wrong. 4:00 - 6:00 PM at The Rabbit Box. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/iceland-unfiltered. Taste of Iceland was created to celebrate and introduce the traditions and culture of Iceland to North America. For more information on Taste of Iceland, please visit https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Saturday, March 21, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Green Issaquah: Restoration Event at Park Pointe
item.description:
Saturday, March 21, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: SE Evans St, Issaquah, WA

Join the Issaquah Alps Trails Club and Green Issaquah on the third Saturday of each month for invasive species removal events at Park Pointe! We will be removing invasive species such as Himalayan blackberry bushes to prepare for our fall planting event. These invasive plants inhibit the growth of native plants, and removing them will ensure the longevity and health of our newest additions. This event is part of our ongoing efforts to help meet the Issaquah Climate Action Plan goal of 55% tree canopy cover by 2035. When and Where Date & Time: Saturday, March 21st, 2025, 9 AM - Noon Where: Park Pointe Where to Park Parking for Park Pointe is available along SE Evans Street, which is overflow for Issaquah High School and the parking for the Sportsman Club \(see inset picture map below\). Click here for parking directions. Where to Meet We will be meeting at the end of the dirt road at the trailhead. What to Bring Bring your own gloves if you have them. There will be gloves available for those who don't have their own. Please dress in layers and in clothing you don't mind getting dirty. Closed-toed shoes are required. Long sleeves and pants, especially denim, are strongly recommended. Bring a full water bottle and snacks. Don't forget to bring your sunscreen! Point of Contact Anne Newcomb \(anne@annenewcomb.net\) and Matt LaBelle \(mattlabelle@hotmail.com\), your work party leaders. Registration required. You can check out our Event FAQs on our website.

item.description:

Description Have stuff to recycle that doesn't go in your cart? Bring it to the West Seattle Recycling & Reuse Collection event on Saturday, March 21 between 9:00 AM and 12:00 PM! We host this event every year to collect items for recycling and reuse that can't go in your home recycle carts or dumpsters. Events are free and open to all Seattle residents. We heard your feedback and made big changes! This year, you'll park your car in the South Seattle College SOUTH parking lot. Volunteers will be available on site to help anyone with mobility needs. Early and late drop-offs will not be accepted, and trucks leave when full. Beyond the Cart Community Reuse & Recycling Event, brought to you by the West Seattle Chamber and the West Seattle Junction Association, in collaboration with Waste Management and Seattle Public Utilities. ACCEPTED ITEMS: Foam blocks & peanuts Household batteries \(no damaged batteries\) Florescent tubes and bulbs \(no broken bulbs, limit: 4 ft.\) Small, empty propane camping canisters Small electronics \(TVs, computers, etc.\) Small appliances \(non-freon\) Paper for shredding \(limit: 4 boxes of paper\) Clothing, accessories, bedding, and curtains in good condition Home goods and toys ITEMS NOT ACCEPTED: Commercial loads Furniture or household goods Garbage Yard waste Household recycling \(items that go in your cart/dumpster\) Hazardous waste Automotive waste Construction waste Non-recyclable or reusable items Car seats Mattresses TO VOLUNTEER FOR THIS EVENT, PLEASE REGISTER USING ACCURATE CONTACT INFORMATION. IF YOU ARE ONLY DROPPING OFF ITEMS, NO REGISTRATION IS NEEDED.

This exciting one-day event will feature activities like West African dance & storytelling, chocolate tasting, Duwamish River tours, local food tasting, arts & crafts, and more. Sign up now for a full day of joy and community! Best Day Ever is a one-day, community-powered experience that celebrates the vibrancy, culture, and pride of Seattle’s neighborhoods. Co-created by the Department of Neighborhoods \(DON\) and the Seattle Chamber of Connection \(SCoC\), Best Day Ever strengthens civic belonging by bringing people, place, and purpose together. Guided by DON’s Community Engagement Framework and the Chamber’s Six Points of Connection, this initiative uplifts community leadership and honors the histories, creativity, and cultural assets that make each neighborhood unique. A thriving neighborhood is one where residents feel connected, represented, and proud of where they live — and Best Day Ever is designed to bring that feeling to life. Each activation builds belonging through service, storytelling, movement, shared meals, joyful connection, and economic visibility for local small businesses.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, March 21, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, March 21, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: BrainFest with Allen Institute
item.description:

Location: Pacific Science Center, 200 Second Ave. North, Seattle, WA

Join us for the third annual BrainFest, a day of brainy fun for all ages at PacSci. Explore hands-on activities, meet Allen Institute scientists and collaborators, learn about cutting-edge neuroscience research, and even touch a real brain! Whether you’re a budding young scientist, a lifelong learner, or simply fascinated by how the mind works, BrainFest 2026 promises a full day of discovery, inspiration, and wonder. Tickets BrainFest is included with General Admission. A limited number of free general admission tickets will be given out on the day of the event on a first-come first-serve basis. This event is a partnership of the Allen Institute and Pacific Science Center.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Young Dragon: A Bruce Lee Story
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Seattle Children's Theatre, 201 Thomas Street, Seattle, WA

Before he was a legend, Bruce Lee was just a young man trying to figure it all out—right here in Seattle. This action-packed, soul-searching story follows the Young Dragon as he navigates a new country and finds his voice. Through movement, magic, and heart, we meet the human behind the icon—flawed, fierce, and full of possibility. Watch as Lee learns to clear his brain by mimicking the white noise of water and begins fusing martial arts with philosophy. From street fights in Hong Kong to teaching self-defense and chasing big dreams, Bruce’s journey reminds us that finding your way means first figuring out who you are. Come see how Seattle shaped him into a flexible, fluid, and flowing master—and how he reshaped the world.

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, March 23, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

Ben Jonson’s renowned 1606 comedy takes us on a wild funny ride with one of the greatest plots in all theatre through a greedy mad society where self-interest is the only real value, and deception the only truth. And where the greatest pleasure of all is…THE GRIFT.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.description:

Mindfulness Walk Taking inspiration from the Japanese idea of shinrin-yoku \(forest bathing\), we will be hosting a series of mindfulness walks in Pioneer Park. Mindfulness walking in the forest has shown to have a myriad of benefits from improving heart health to reducing stress \(https://www.npr.org/2023/08/22/1195337204/a-guide-to-forest-bathing\). Forest Steward Marilyn Cornwell \(https://www.soulscapes.us/\) will be lea ding an hour-long guided walk through the park. Please join us if your cur ious about mindfulness, want to learn more about and experience healing powers of being in nature, and/or if you want to deepen your connection with Pioneer Park and others. When: Tuesday, March 24th, 11am-12pm Where: Pioneer Park SE, Meet at the corner of Island Crest Way and SE 68th St. Follow VOLUNTEER EVENT Signs What to bring: Dress for the weather in clothes and shoes you will comfortable walking and standing in, as well as water. item.title: MOHAI Mercantile Literary Series - Seattle's Locks and Ship Canal
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

Register ➞ https://bit.ly/3NbLLJX MOHAI Mercantile is proud to host the official book launch event for Seattle’s Locks and Ship Canal with local historians David B. Williams and Jennifer Ott! This portable guide from UW Press and HistoryLink is an introduction to the engineering marvel of the Ballard locks, and a primer on the history of the ship canal. Hear from the authors about the tumultuous history of one of Seattle’s defining projects, and the dramatic changes it brought to the city’s economy, neighborhoods, and natural environment. Doors open at 6 pm for music and mingling. Food and drink available for purchase. Conversation and Q&A begin at 7 pm, followed by a book signing with the authors.

item.title: IMPROV 100 TUESDAY - Intro to Improv: Say Yes!, SPRING'26
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Unexpected Productions, 1428 Post Alley, Seattle, WA

Say yes! Learn to listen, connect, play and use your own life to create stories with others! An 8 week intro to improvised theatre! Discover Unexpected Productions' philosophy of improvisation, developed over our 43 years of improvising in Seattle! You'll learn how to actively listen, to use your own life to create collaborative stories, and you'll work on the fundamental principles of improvisation: what it means to say, "Yes, And", how to tell stories that include everyone in the room, how to stop worrying about failing, and the games and concepts behind what we do on stages around the world! Under the guidance of one of our best Seattle improv teachers, you will learn to:· -Be a Better Listener -Conquer Fears -Spark Creativity -Meet New People & Make New Friends -Become a Better Problem Solver -Gain Confidence· -Fail With Grace \(and Maybe Even Enjoy It\) -Say Yes More in Your Life -Get Out of Your Comfort Zone -Become a Better Public Speaker -Discover More About Yourself -...and have a blast doing it! Time and Dates : Tuesdays for eight \(8\) weeks, from March 24 to May 22, 7pm to 9:30pm NOTE: Attendance at the first session of your course is mandatory as it sets the agenda for the entire quarter. If you think you will have to miss the first class, please consider registering for the next quarter. No Prerequisites. All students begin at this level, even those with existing improv experience. It’s a prerequisite to the other levels. Location: 5700 6th Ave St, Suite 109, Umbrella Room, Seattle, WA 98108 \(In Georgetown, south of downtown, with plenty of parking.\) Maximum Students: 16 Cost: Ticket price $295.00 Age: 18 and above \*\*Cancellation policy: If you must cancel before the first class, we can only offer credit towards the same level class next quarter. We are unable to offer refunds. If you cancel after the first class we cannot offer credit towards a future class. Again, we are unable to offer refunds.

item.title: Chuckle Bucket Tuesday @ Club Comedy Seattle (Open Mic)
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Club Comedy Seattle, 328 15th Ave E, Seattle, WA

Club Comedy Seattle hosts Chuckle Bucket Tuesday each week. The luck of the bucket rules the night. Comics drop their name in and hope to be drawn for one of the spots on the list to tell jokes. It's fun, fast-paced and will keep you laughing hard on your Tuesday night. Door 7:00PM/Show 7:30PM Admission: $7 \+ $0.35 City Of Seattle Admission Tax 21\+ With Valid ID REQUIRED for entry. NO REFUNDS. FYI: If you have a large party it's easier for us to seat you all together if you purchase all the tickets under one name. . Club Comedy Seattle FAQ Is there an age/ID requirement? Yes. We are strictly 21\+ and valid physical ID is REQUIRED For Entry Is Seating First Come/First Served? Yes. The room is all two top tables and we seat guests in the order in which they arrive. Can I reserve a table for a group? We do not take reservations for tables, but groups can arrive early to secure seating together. Are tickets available at the door if an event is sold out online? If an event is sold out online there are no tickets available at the door. What are the club’s hours of operation? The club is open Tuesday to Sunday. Doors open a half hour before each show. What is the Parking like? Generally there is street parking available in the blocks surrounding the club. There are also affordable parking lots on 16th Ave E right around the block from the club along with other easy options in the neighborhood Is there a dress code? There is no formal dress code, but we recommend smart casual attire. Can I bring food or drinks? Outside food and drinks are not allowed. We have a full bar and a menu available for your enjoyment. What if I can't make it and need to cancel my ticket? All ticket sales are final. However, if you can’t make it, please contact us through Eventbrite in advance, and we’ll do our best to accommodate you. Will there be an opener for the headliner? Yes, headliner shows feature a lineup of comedians, including a host, an opener, and the headliner. Will the comics talk to me like I see in online videos all the time? Most likely they won't. Crowd work videos are popular for posting online, but crowd work doesn't generally happen at the online frequency in live comedy show situations. Some comics do engage the crowd as part of their regular set and it's generally always fun and more about making fun of themselves than making fun of the crowd. How can I get on the Cheap Laughs Open Mic On Wednesdays? Submissions for spots on the Wednesday Cheap Laughs Open Mic are accepted only on the Tuesday before between 12:00AM and 11:59PM via email at openmic@clubcomedyseattle.com Do you offer group discounts? Yes, we offer discounts for groups of ten or more. Please contact us for more details. Can I meet the comedians after the show? Meet-and-greet opportunities vary by event. Please ask staff for details after the show.

Event details
