Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Jazz Month Tour
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 7:30 AM

Location: The Royal Room, 5000 Rainier Ave South, Seattle, WA

March’s Women in Jazz Month comes alive as award-winning singer-songwriter Eugenie Jones lights up the Royal Room with her national Women in Jazz Month Tour. With stops in Denver, San Francisco, Atlanta, and Seattle, this tour joins a worldwide tribute to the women who shaped jazz, honoring trailblazers such as Billie Holiday, Nina Simone, and Ella Fitzgerald. At the Royal Room, Jones will blend beloved songs from female jazz legends, her own Jazz Week \#4 ranked originals, imaginative spins on vintage jazz, and timeless rhythm and blues classics. She’ll be joined by the remarkable talents of pianist Peter Adams, bassist Osama Afifi, and drummer Maria Wulff. As a Seattle Jazz Hall of Fame inductee, the first vocal recording artist to receive Earshot Jazz’s Album of the Year award, a two-time Vocalist of the Year winner, and a Jazz Journalists Association’s Jazz Hero Award winner, Jones has received high praise for her vocal prowess. Paris Move magazine describes Jones as “One of the most beautiful voices in the United States; a consummate interpreter who slips effortlessly into the soul of her songs.” Preview Jones’ music, watch videos, or learn more about this gifted entertainer at https://ffm.to/eugeniejonesmusic. Get your tickets today for this unforgettable night of exquisite vocals, masterful musicianship, and stirring storytelling, all in tribute to the enduring legacy and vibrant contributions of women in jazz. Tickets: https://www.strangertickets.com/events/180924339/eugenie-jones

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 11 AM

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: BiggerPockets Value-Add Conference
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 8 AM

Location: Seattle, Wa, 98121, Seattle, WA

Real estate investors looking to sharpen their execution and scale for smarter deals will not want to miss the **first-ever BiggerPockets Value-Add Conference** in Seattle on March 28, 2026. This one-day, tight-knit event has only 120 seats available and is designed specifically for experienced investors actively executing deals. Led by seasoned local investor and Million Dollar Zombie Flips host James Dainard, alongside BiggerPockets experts Dave Meyer and Henry Washington, the conference offers a rare opportunity to learn how top investors scale deals in today’s competitive real estate market through stronger underwriting, strategic renovations, optimized financing, and disciplined execution systems. Investment to attend the one-day conference starts at $500, with a limited number of spots available HERE.  The in-person event features powerful speakers, delivering  practical, action-based strategies investors can implement immediately, including: Team building and legal structure for sustainable growth Underwriting and deal structuring across asset types Capital stack and financing strategies (hard money, DSCR, BRRRR) Construction and project management systems that protect margins Renovations and design decisions that directly impact ROI Scaling operations with stronger vendor management and streamlined processes This experience is designed for investors with three or more completed deals who are actively executing BRRRRs, flips, or small-scale development projects and looking to build repeatable, scalable systems. With Seattle’s competitive housing market and continued investor activity, the conference offers timely insight into how local operators are navigating tighter margins, rising costs, and evolving financing strategies. In addition to the conference, on March 29, a limited Premium Property Tour will take attendees inside active Seattle investment properties, where James and the speakers will walk through real deals and answer questions in small groups.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: Homebuyer Education Workshop [VIRTUAL]
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

This is a free five-hour workshop to help you meet the WA State Housing Finance Commission’s education requirements for down payment assistance and a variety of affordable housing programs and opportunities for qualified applicants! These requirements include the successful completion of a Homebuyers Education Seminar meeting the current FHA, Fannie Mae, FreddieMac, and SMBA benchmark standards.  At the conclusion of the workshop, participants will receive a certificate of achievement from the WA State Housing Finance Commission and DFI valid for two years from the date of issuance.  To join our next workshop, please complete and submit the following registration form at least 24-hours before your selected workshop date. Once this form has been submitted, be sure to check your email for a digital confirmation, as well as additional follow up and important information from a ULMS Housing Department staff member.   Virtual workshops are held every 3rd and 4th Saturday of the month from 10:00AM – 3:30PM. A Zoom link will be sent to you via a ULMS Housing department staff member upon completing registration.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Restoration Event | Pioneer Park
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Pioneer Park Clean Up! Join us for a Forest Steward led wo rk party in Pioneer Park SE. For this work party volunteers will be removi ng ivy that is blanketing the forest floor. Removing ivy helps to increase the biodiversity of park by making room for native plants to sprout up. I f left unchecked, ivy will grow over our native plants and eventually pull them to the ground. Your help in removing ivy will play a pivotal role i n creating a healthier future for the park! No experience necessary, all ages welcome. Please note: we require participants aged 14 and under to volunteer with a parent or guardian at a ratio of 1 adult to 3 kids. When: Saturday, March 28th, 10am-12pm Where: Pioneer Park SE, a map will be provided for directions to the specific work site. What to bring : Dress for the weather in clothes you don't mind getting dirty \(think: lo ng pants, boots or sneakers\). Bring water. Small snacks, gloves, and tools will be provided. Registration: https://volunteersignup.org/CWRCR

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Seed Starting Tips and Techniques
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Bellevue Botanical Garden, 12001 Main St, Bellevue, WA

Taught by Nancy Kartes and Bill Thorness. Our mild late winter allows us to sprout the edible garden early. Getting growing now will mean longer harvests and more use of our garden throughout the year. Learn what can be started now, whether to choose seeds or starts, where to site your early spring garden, how to know when the soil is ready, and how to plan ahead for the best success. Hands-on lessons will also teach you how to make a “seed snail,” how to use a soil block, and how to make your own seed-starting mix! You’ll also go home with some early-season seeds ready to sprout. $24.50 BBGS Member/$35 Non-Member.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: PCC Cooking Class - Savory Sichuan
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Columbia City PCC, 3610 S Edmunds St, Seattle, WA

Learn to make traditional Chinese dishes at home! In this cooking class, you’ll pick up techniques and tips for capturing the flavors of Sichuan cuisine. Get practice infusing oil with chilis and hand-grinding spices for a sauce as you create deeply flavorful dan dan noodles. You’ll also try your hand at methodically rolling and flattening dough with scallions for savory pancakes, which you’ll pair with a scratch-made dipping sauce. By the end of class, you’ll be ready to recreate the entire menu in your kitchen.  Contains pork and dairy.  About the instructor: Our seasoned instructors and chefs have been chosen to be a part of PCC Cooks because of their culinary expertise and sincere enthusiasm and talent for teaching.  This is a hands-on format class for adults.  This cooking class is available at multiple PCC locations and dates. See below for details: Bellevue PCC -Friday, March 27, 6:00 - 8:00 pm  -Sunday, March 29, 10:00 - 12:00 pm  -Friday, April 3, 10:00 - 12:00 pm  Bothell PCC -Friday, March 27, 6:00 - 8:00 pm  -Sunday, March 29, 10:00 - 12:00 pm  Burien PCC -Sunday, March 29, 10:00 - 12:00 pm  Columbia City PCC -Sunday, March 29, 10:00 - 12:00 pm  Edmonds PCC -Friday, March 27, 6:00 - 8:00 pm  -Sunday, March 29, 10:00 - 12:00 pm  Green Lake Village PCC -Friday, March 27, 6:00 - 8:00 pm  -Sunday, March 29, 10:00 - 12:00 pm

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Natasha Zimmers with Naja Lund Aparico - 'Tiny Hiker' and 'Seasons by the Lake: Adventures in Greenl
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Third Place Books Lake Forest Park, 17171 Bothell Way NE,, Lake Forest Park, WA

Welcome spring by celebrating the joy of exploring the great outdoors! Third Place Books welcomes authors Natasha Zimmers and Naja Lund Aparico to our Lake Forest Park store for a storytime featuring Natasha's book Tiny Hikers and Naja's book Seasons by the Lake: Adventures in Greenland. This event is free and open to the public. RSVP is not required. Readers of all ages welcome! About Tiny Hikers . . . "Offers a realistic, joyful depiction of a young adventurer and reflects the author's experience as an outdoor educator. A standout debut that will inspire young readers to explore the outdoors." -- School Library Journal \(SLJ\), starred review The tiny hiker finishes loading up her pack and excitedly gets on the trail, leading her family through the ancient trees toward their beach campsite. At first it's fun to explore--she finds a hollow log, wriggles under a fallen tree, and spots a slimy slug But then the trail starts getting steeper, and when her legs get tired, she trips on a root and takes a hard fall. Now her knee hurts, her elbows hurt...everything hurts She just wants to give up. But after a little break, a tasty snack and an exciting glimpse of the beach, the tiny hiker feels better and decides she will make it. With one final scamper over some giant beach logs, she finds the beach. Time to play And at the end of the long day, after sitting by the campfire, the tiny hiker gets cozy in her sleeping bag and falls fast asleep. About Seasons by the Lake. . . Spend a year experiencing the seasons in Greenland with two Inuit siblings. Brothers Minik and Nuka have a whole year of adventures ahead in Greenland, including finding ice shards from glaciers in the spring to picking crowberries during the summer when the sun barely sets. The darkness of winter reveals Arsarnerit, or the Aurora Borealis, above as the two whisper about the Inuit myth behind the lights. Greenlandic Inuk author Naja Lund Aparico combines a sweet sibling story with an exploration of the seasons in the Arctic featuring stunning illustrations by German and Choctaw illustrator Alex Nees that captures the sweeping landscape. Natasha Zimmers loves being outside in all seasons and weather, but her favourite place to be is on a wild west coast beach, backpacking with her own not-so-tiny hikers. She teaches outdoor education and loves seeing the world through the eyes of her students as they discover the wonders of nature. Naja Lund Aparico is a Greenlandic Inuk author with a background in international relations and currently a second year MFA graduate student at the Institute of American Indian Arts \(IAIA\) with a focus on creative non-fiction. Having grown up in Greenland, she now lives in the Pacific Northwest with her family. Aparico’s children’s books and essays are centered on the indigenous identity with an emphasis on kin, heritage, and the environment. Her US debut picture book, SEASONS BY THE LAKE, will be published by Dial / PRH in March of 2026, while her second picture book \(untitled\) is set to be published by Candlewick in late Spring of 2027, and her third picture book will be published by Dial/PRH in late 2027. Want a signed edition of the featured book, but can't make it to the event? Order through our website or over the phone, and write your request for a signature or personalization in the comments field at checkout. Please call the hosting store if you're placing your order within 24 hours of the event. For media or access inquiries, please email events@thirdplacebooks.com or call our Lake Forest Park store at \(206\) 366-3311.

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.description:

Baker/North Rainier,North Beach/Blue Ridge,North Beacon Hill/Jefferson Park,North Capitol Hill ,North of Seattle,Northgate/Maple Leaf,Online Event,Pioneer Square,Q ueen Anne,Rainier Beach/Othello,Ravenna/Bryant/Roosevelt,Riverview,R oxhill/Westwood/White Center,Seward Park,South Beacon Hill/New Holly, South of Seattle,South Park,Sunset Hill/Loyal Heights,University Dist rict,Uptown,Wallingford,Wedgwood/View Ridge,West of Seattle,West Se attle Junction/Genesee Hill,Whittier Heights Event Types: Arts,Comm unity,Dance,Theater,Youth Audience: Adults,All,Children,Family ,Senior,Special Needs,Teen Sponsoring Organization: Variety Plus Th eater Contact: Angela Rinaldi Contact Phone: \(206\) 819-6039 Contact Email: vptheater@aol.com Pre-Register: No Cost: $20 General Admission Registration Details: Online tickets at: https://www.varietyplus.org/performancesTickets also available at the door Link: https://www.varietyplus.org A new musical adaptation! Featuring 33 Seattle area youth Based on the book: Tammy the Troll, A Dance in the Forest by Dina McDermott Written and directed by Bob Gribas Original Music by Angela Rinaldi-Gribas Choreography by Dina McDermott The Story A curious group of friends ventures into the heart of the forest in search of Tammy the Troll-a shy legend whispered about but never truly known. Their path winds beneath glistening spider webs, through knotted roots and towering mountains, and along the swif t, laughing currents of the river. Guided and aided by the forest's magical residents-Spiders, Talking Trees, the Foothill Folk , clever Mice, and playful River Tributaries-the friends press on. Every challenge draws them closer, proving that the greatest reward of the journey is not just reaching Tammy, but discovering the beginnings of a lasting friendship.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Monday, March 30, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, March 30, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: The Early November & Hellogoodbye
item.description:
Monday, March 30, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: The Showbox, 1426 1st Ave., Seattle, WA

Six years have passed since New Jersey's The Early November released what, at the time, appeared to be their final album. Sprawling across three discs, The Mother, The Mechanic & The Path was a conceptual, grandiose and challenging record. Challenging for listeners but even more so, for the band that created it. Plagued with recording and creative struggles, it left the band feeling drained and frustrated. More so than anyone, it left songwriter and front-man, Ace Enders, feeling defeated. While proud of the record and response, he knew that it was not what he originally dreamed it would be. Less than seven months after it's release, the band decided to call it day and announced their "indefinite hiatus" in March of 2007. Since then, much has happened. Enders immediately set out with a new solo moniker, Ace Enders & A Million Different People, determined to do everything "right" this time. In the music industry, that includes going to Hollywood to work with a hotshot producer and make a "hit" record. A deeply personal and sensitive songwriter, Enders found the experience less than gratifying to have his songs manipulated and contorted. As someone who has always produced their own material, the last straw came when he was asked to leave his own session only to come back and find a song completely re-arranged and re-recorded. The final product was ultimately scrapped and Enders vowed to never again make a record on someone else's terms. After re-gaining his confidence and recording independence, he went on to release multiple fulllengths and EP's under multiple project names \(…And A Million Different People, I Can Make A Mess Like Nobody's Business, solo\) while keeping a continuous tour schedule. The other members stayed active as well. Guitarist Joseph Marro moved to California and joined indie-pop band HelloGoodbye. Drummer Jeff Kummer released a solo album, completed college and began working in television production. Bassist Sergio Anello toured with Enders for a period. Guitarist William Lugg worked in organic farming and even dug graves at a point. While still remaining close friends, the members appeared to have completely moved on from the band. Just a couple months before they all took the stage again at the sold-out Electric Factory in Philadelphia, an Early November reunion seemed to be about the most unlikely thing to would happen. Even before playing a second time, the old friends knew that they wanted to make a record together and be a band again. Things had changed for the little underdog band from the farming town of Hammonton, NJ during all those years. They found themselves with new purpose, drive and knowledge. Having signed their first recording contract at the ripe, old ages of 17, 18 & 19, they knew what they did and did not want to do this time around. They found the perfect partner in Portland, OR label Rise Records and set out to record a new album in Ace Enders recently built, Living Room Recording studio with Enders at the helm. In Currents is a record that could have only been made by this band, at this specific point in time. The Early November had to have the experiences they did in the past 10 years which are documented on this new album. The good and the bad, the ups and the downs. The tastes of success and the crushing disappointments. The band has grown up, lived life and seen both its beauty and dismay. An atmosphere and tension hovers over the entire record leaving one unsure whether they should smile or sigh. In Currents is an introspective and thought provoking record displaying a gamut of emotional territory. In the opening track, A Stain On The Carpet, Enders recollects a regretful evening and confronts the threat of losing someone to dementia. The alt-country tinged, The Smell Of This Place, reminds himself and his loved ones that those very ups and downs have been worth the ride and are responsible for where and who they are now. In the short but poignant Digital Age, Enders addresses an issue facing every musician; how to survive in a industry that seems to be abandoning its art. With their first new record in six years, The Early November have made an album that strongly and positively represents their entire career. The raw and youthful attitude from 2003's, The Rooms Too Cold blends with the ambitious, sonic experimentation of The Mother, The Mechanic & The Path. Enders describes In Currents as "a journey through life's joys and struggles" and "being pulled in a direction you can't control…like an ocean current or the flow of electricity". It's a scenario everyone in this world is in and can relate to. It's not about where those currents will eventually take you, it's about the journey you'll have and the experiences you'll take with you that matter the most.

Event details

item.title: Voxtrot
item.description:
Monday, March 30, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Neumos, 925 E Pike St, Seattle, WA

Neumos Presents VOXTROT MON, 30 MAR 2026 at 07:00PM PDT Ages: All Ages to Enter, 21 & Over to Drink Doors Open: 07:00PM OnSale: Fri, 5 Dec 2025 at 10:00AM PST Announcement: Tue, 2 Dec 2025 at 07:00AM PST Voxtrot first captured global attention in the 2000s blog era with a pair of beloved EPs \(Raised By Wolves and Mothers, Sisters, Daughters & Wives\), released on their own Cult Hero Records label. That momentum carried into 2006’s Your Biggest Fan EP, which debuted at \#3 on the Billboard Hot Singles Sales chart, and their 2007 self-titled full-length on Playlouder/Beggars, which reached \#14 on Billboard’s Heatseekers. Their rise was swift, their impact immediate, and their disbandment in 2010 left a devoted audience to keep the music alive. Over the following decade, that audience only grew—proof that Voxtrot’s songs had outlived the moment that birthed them. Now, after more than a decade apart, Voxtrot has reconvened with a renewed sense of purpose. Dreamers in Exile, coming via Cult Hero, is the band’s first full-length in nearly twenty years. Written and self-recorded in Texas at bassist Jason Chronis’ Haunted Air Studio, and mixed by Dean Reid \(Lana Del Rey, James Blake, The Black Keys\), the album captures a group both wiser and bolder. Where the early records chronicled the wide-eyed tumult of youth, Dreamers in Exile is an album of reckoning and renewal, tracing the passage of time, the distance between past and present, and the hope of connection that endures through it all. It is at once a continuation and a reinvention—the sound of Voxtrot returning not to reclaim their legacy, but to expand it. Important Information: \* This event is all ages. Valid ID is required to enter bar areas. \* Doors open at the listed event time. \* Event start time is generally one hour after doors open but is subject to change without notice. \* All tickets are nonrefundable with the exception of event cancellation. \* Support acts are subject to change. \* Orders in violation of the published ticket limit are subject to cancellation without notice.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: THE MAINE
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 5:30 PM

Location: Showbox SoDo, 1700 1st Ave. South, Seattle, WA

Seven albums in, and The Maine's new release You Are Ok is arguably the band's most considerable sonic leap in their twelve-year career. What makes The Maine, The Maine is their ability and inspiration to take risks, all while staying tuned in and consistently exceeding the expectations of their fans. 2019 is shaping up to be another milestone year for these desert dwellers, which kicked off with their second ever headlining music festival in their hometown. You Are Ok marks The Maine's first top 10 album in its premiere on the charts \(peaking at \#8\), and also came in at \#1 independent record, \#2 rock album, \#2 vinyl album, and \#3 alternative album. Known for their live show, The Maine is taking You Are Ok around the globe this year including stops on their home continent, across South America, Europe, and more. The band will spend their summer on the Sad Summer Festival, co-headlining the inaugural year of a touring music festival in the US. The Maine is John O'Callaghan, Pat Kirch, Jared Monaco, Garrett Nickelsen, and Kennedy Brock.

item.title: Maddox Batson
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Crystal Ballroom, Seattle, WA

Maddox Batson, a teen sensation born in Nashville and raised in Birmingham, Alabama, combines modern neo-traditional Country music with a touch of southern rock and pop. He's a versatile talent, excelling as a singer/songwriter, multi-instrument musician, actor and athlete. Batson's humor and charm shine through his viral social media videos, amassing over 200M views in under five months and over 3M followers. Born to entertain, he brings a unique style that sets him apart. Touted as a "mix between Cole Swindell, Morgan Wallen and Justin Bieber," he is continuing to hone his craft, both as a vocalist and as a songwriter for his ever-growing fan base and hopes to win you over with his genuine smile and infectious voice. In addition to his two viral track releases "Tears In The River" and "I Wanna Know," Batson has also solidified his spot in the writers room, most recently landing a cut from global superstars Lana Del Ray and Quavo with "Tough." The rising star will join Gabby Barrett on stage this August with more dates to be announced.

item.title: Chuckle Bucket Tuesday @ Club Comedy Seattle (Open Mic)
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Club Comedy Seattle, 328 15th Ave E, Seattle, WA

Club Comedy Seattle hosts Chuckle Bucket Tuesday each week. The luck of the bucket rules the night. Comics drop their name in and hope to be drawn for one of the spots on the list to tell jokes. It's fun, fast-paced and will keep you laughing hard on your Tuesday night. Door 7:00PM/Show 7:30PM Admission: $7 \+ $0.35 City Of Seattle Admission Tax 21\+ With Valid ID REQUIRED for entry. NO REFUNDS. FYI: If you have a large party it's easier for us to seat you all together if you purchase all the tickets under one name. . Club Comedy Seattle FAQ Is there an age/ID requirement? Yes. We are strictly 21\+ and valid physical ID is REQUIRED For Entry Is Seating First Come/First Served? Yes. The room is all two top tables and we seat guests in the order in which they arrive. Can I reserve a table for a group? We do not take reservations for tables, but groups can arrive early to secure seating together. Are tickets available at the door if an event is sold out online? If an event is sold out online there are no tickets available at the door. What are the club’s hours of operation? The club is open Tuesday to Sunday. Doors open a half hour before each show. What is the Parking like? Generally there is street parking available in the blocks surrounding the club. There are also affordable parking lots on 16th Ave E right around the block from the club along with other easy options in the neighborhood Is there a dress code? There is no formal dress code, but we recommend smart casual attire. Can I bring food or drinks? Outside food and drinks are not allowed. We have a full bar and a menu available for your enjoyment. What if I can't make it and need to cancel my ticket? All ticket sales are final. However, if you can’t make it, please contact us through Eventbrite in advance, and we’ll do our best to accommodate you. Will there be an opener for the headliner? Yes, headliner shows feature a lineup of comedians, including a host, an opener, and the headliner. Will the comics talk to me like I see in online videos all the time? Most likely they won't. Crowd work videos are popular for posting online, but crowd work doesn't generally happen at the online frequency in live comedy show situations. Some comics do engage the crowd as part of their regular set and it's generally always fun and more about making fun of themselves than making fun of the crowd. How can I get on the Cheap Laughs Open Mic On Wednesdays? Submissions for spots on the Wednesday Cheap Laughs Open Mic are accepted only on the Tuesday before between 12:00AM and 11:59PM via email at openmic@clubcomedyseattle.com Do you offer group discounts? Yes, we offer discounts for groups of ten or more. Please contact us for more details. Can I meet the comedians after the show? Meet-and-greet opportunities vary by event. Please ask staff for details after the show.
